notes.heading: Need for Displacement Current
notes.text: Ampere’s circuital law in its original form was incomplete for situations where electric field changes with time, especially during the charging of a capacitor.
points: Original Ampere’s circuital law: ∮ B · dl = μ₀I.
This law says that an electric current produces a magnetic field.
During charging of a capacitor, current flows in the connecting wires and charges collect on the capacitor plates.
Between the capacitor plates there is an insulating gap, so no conduction current flows through the gap.
Outside the capacitor, conduction current exists, but between the plates conduction current is zero.
Magnetic field cannot suddenly disappear between the plates. This creates a contradiction in the original Ampere’s law.
Maxwell proposed that the changing electric field between the capacitor plates behaves like a current.
This current due to changing electric flux is called displacement current.
Formula for displacement current: I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).
Here, I_d is displacement current, ε₀ is permittivity of free space, Φ_E is electric flux, and dΦ_E/dt is the rate of change of electric flux.
Corrected Ampere-Maxwell law: ∮ B · dl = μ₀(I_c + I_d).
Here, I_c is conduction current and I_d is displacement current.
A magnetic field is produced not only by conduction current but also by changing electric field.
In a charging capacitor, in the wires I_c ≠ 0, between the plates I_c = 0, but I_d ≠ 0.
Displacement current is not actual flow of charges. It is due to changing electric field.
Important idea: A changing electric field produces a magnetic field.

notes.heading: Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves
notes.text: Maxwell combined the laws of electricity and magnetism and showed that electric and magnetic fields are connected. A changing electric field produces a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field produces an electric field.
points: Because changing electric and magnetic fields produce each other, they can travel through space as a wave.
This wave is called an electromagnetic wave.
An oscillating electric charge produces a changing electric field.
The changing electric field produces a changing magnetic field.
The changing magnetic field again produces a changing electric field.
This continuous process produces an electromagnetic wave.
An electromagnetic wave contains both electric field E and magnetic field B.
The electric field and magnetic field are perpendicular to each other.
Both E and B are perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave.
Therefore, electromagnetic waves are transverse waves.
If E is along the x-axis and B is along the y-axis, the wave travels along the z-axis.
For electromagnetic waves: E ⟂ B, E ⟂ direction of propagation, and B ⟂ direction of propagation.
Speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum: c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀).
Value of speed of light: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
Maxwell concluded that light is an electromagnetic wave.
Relation between maximum electric field and maximum magnetic field: E₀/B₀ = c or E₀ = cB₀.
Here, E₀ is maximum electric field and B₀ is maximum magnetic field.

notes.heading: Properties of Electromagnetic Waves
notes.text: Electromagnetic waves include light, radio waves, microwaves, X-rays, gamma rays and other radiations. They have common wave properties and can travel through vacuum.
points: Electromagnetic waves do not need a material medium for propagation.
Mechanical waves like sound need a medium, but electromagnetic waves can travel through vacuum.
Example: Sunlight reaches Earth through empty space.
In vacuum, all electromagnetic waves travel with speed c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
Electromagnetic waves are transverse waves.
In an electromagnetic wave, E ⟂ B and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
Electric and magnetic fields are in the same phase.
This means E and B reach maximum values at the same time and become zero at the same time.
Electromagnetic waves carry energy from one place to another.
Example: Sunlight carries energy from the Sun to Earth.
Example: Microwaves carry energy to heat food.
Electromagnetic waves also carry momentum.
When electromagnetic radiation falls on a surface, it can exert pressure called radiation pressure.
Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerated charges.
A charge at rest produces only electric field.
A charge moving with uniform velocity produces electric and magnetic fields.
An accelerated charge produces electromagnetic waves.
Electromagnetic waves show reflection, refraction, interference, diffraction and polarisation.
For an electromagnetic wave in vacuum: E₀/B₀ = c or E₀ = cB₀.
Frequency-wavelength relation: c = νλ.
Here, c is speed of light, ν is frequency and λ is wavelength.
If frequency increases, wavelength decreases.

notes.heading: Electromagnetic Spectrum
notes.text: The electromagnetic spectrum is the complete range of electromagnetic waves arranged according to frequency or wavelength.
points: All electromagnetic waves travel with the same speed in vacuum: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
Different electromagnetic waves differ in wavelength λ and frequency ν.
Frequency and wavelength are related by c = νλ.
If frequency increases, wavelength decreases.
Order from long wavelength to short wavelength: Radio waves → Microwaves → Infrared rays → Visible light → Ultraviolet rays → X-rays → Gamma rays.
Memory trick: R M I V U X G = Raju Makes Idli Very Useful Xtra Good.
Radio waves have the longest wavelength and lowest frequency.
Radio waves are used in radio broadcasting, television transmission and mobile communication.
Radio waves are produced by oscillating electric circuits.
Microwaves have shorter wavelength than radio waves.
Microwaves are used in radar systems, microwave ovens and satellite communication.
In microwave ovens, microwaves heat food by making water molecules vibrate.
Infrared rays are also called heat rays.
Infrared rays are emitted by hot bodies.
Infrared rays are used in remote controls, thermal imaging cameras and night vision devices.
Visible light is the part of the electromagnetic spectrum visible to the human eye.
Visible colours are VIBGYOR: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange and Red.
Violet has the shortest wavelength in visible light.
Red has the longest wavelength in visible light.
Ultraviolet rays have higher frequency than visible light.
Ultraviolet rays are used for killing germs, sterilisation and detecting fake currency.
Too much ultraviolet radiation can harm skin and eyes.
X-rays have very short wavelength and high frequency.
X-rays can pass through soft tissues but are absorbed more by bones.
X-rays are used in medical imaging, detecting fractures and security scanning.
Gamma rays have the shortest wavelength and highest frequency.
Gamma rays are emitted in nuclear reactions and radioactive decay.
Gamma rays are used in cancer treatment, sterilisation of medical equipment and nuclear studies.
From radio waves to gamma rays: wavelength decreases, frequency increases and energy increases.
Energy of radiation: E = hν.
Gamma rays have the highest energy.

notes.heading: Radio Waves
notes.text: Radio waves are electromagnetic waves having very long wavelength and low frequency. They form the first part of the electromagnetic spectrum.
points: Order in spectrum: Radio waves → Microwaves → Infrared rays → Visible light → Ultraviolet rays → X-rays → Gamma rays.
Radio waves have the longest wavelength among electromagnetic waves.
Radio waves are produced by accelerated charges in conducting wires.
In practice, radio waves are produced by oscillating electric circuits.
When electric charges oscillate rapidly in an antenna, they produce radio waves.
Simple relation: Oscillating charge → Radio wave.
Radio waves have large wavelength and small frequency.
Using c = νλ, if wavelength is large, frequency is small.
Radio waves are used in radio broadcasting such as AM and FM radio.
Television signals are transmitted using radio waves.
Mobile communication uses radio waves for sending and receiving signals.
Radio waves are also used in wireless communication, walkie-talkies and wireless networks.
Radio waves can travel long distances.
Radio waves can bend around obstacles to some extent due to diffraction.
Example: A radio station converts sound into electrical signals. These signals produce radio waves. A radio receiver catches the waves and converts them back into sound.

notes.heading: Microwaves
notes.text: Microwaves are electromagnetic waves with wavelength shorter than radio waves but longer than infrared rays.
points: Order in spectrum: Radio waves → Microwaves → Infrared rays.
Microwaves have shorter wavelength and higher frequency than radio waves.
Using c = νλ, when wavelength decreases, frequency increases.
Microwaves are produced by special vacuum tubes and electronic devices such as magnetron, klystron and Gunn diode.
These devices produce very high-frequency electromagnetic waves.
Microwaves are used in RADAR.
RADAR means Radio Detection and Ranging.
Radar is used to detect aircraft, ships, speed of vehicles and weather conditions.
Microwaves are useful in radar because they can be sent in a narrow beam and reflected from objects.
Microwaves are used in microwave ovens for cooking food.
In a microwave oven, microwaves make water molecules in food vibrate rapidly.
This vibration produces heat and food gets heated.
Foods containing more water heat faster in microwave ovens.
Microwaves are used in satellite communication because they can pass through Earth’s atmosphere easily.
Microwaves are used for TV signals, telephone signals, internet communication and GPS communication.
Mobile phones and towers also use microwave frequency signals for communication.
Microwaves can be directed in a narrow beam because of their short wavelength.

notes.heading: Infrared Rays
notes.text: Infrared rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelength longer than visible light but shorter than microwaves. They are also called heat rays.
points: Order in spectrum: Microwaves → Infrared rays → Visible light.
Infrared rays are called heat rays because hot bodies emit infrared radiation.
Examples of sources: Sun, heated iron rod, fire and human body.
When infrared rays fall on skin, we feel warmth.
Infrared rays have wavelength shorter than microwaves and longer than visible light.
Their frequency is higher than microwaves but lower than visible light.
Infrared rays are produced by hot bodies, molecules, and atoms during vibration and rotation.
Any object having temperature above absolute zero emits infrared radiation.
Infrared rays are used in TV remotes and AC remotes to send signals.
Infrared cameras detect heat emitted by objects.
Infrared rays are used in night vision cameras, security systems, rescue operations and medical temperature scanning.
Infrared lamps are used in physiotherapy for heat treatment.
Infrared heat treatment helps reduce pain and improve blood circulation.
Weather satellites use infrared radiation to study cloud patterns and temperature distribution.
Infrared rays can pass through fog and mist better than visible light, so they are useful in special photography.
Human eyes cannot see infrared rays, but we can feel them as heat.

notes.heading: Visible Light
notes.text: Visible light is the part of the electromagnetic spectrum that can be detected by the human eye. It is only a small part of the complete electromagnetic spectrum.
points: Order in spectrum: Infrared rays → Visible light → Ultraviolet rays.
Visible light consists of seven colours: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange and Red.
The order VIBGYOR is from shorter wavelength to longer wavelength.
Approximate wavelength range of visible light: 400 nm to 700 nm.
In metre: 4 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7 × 10⁻⁷ m.
Violet light has the shortest wavelength in the visible region.
Red light has the longest wavelength in the visible region.
Using c = νλ, if wavelength is small, frequency is high.
Violet light has the highest frequency and highest energy among visible colours.
Red light has the lowest frequency and lowest energy among visible colours.
Visible light is produced when atoms and molecules release energy.
Sources of visible light include the Sun, electric bulb, tube light, LED and fire.
Visible light helps us see objects.
When light falls on an object and reflects into our eyes, we see the object.
Plants use visible light from the Sun to prepare food by photosynthesis.
Visible light is used in microscopes, telescopes, cameras, spectacles and projectors.
Visible light is also used in optical fibre communication and laser communication.
White light is a mixture of all visible colours.
When white light passes through a prism, it splits into seven colours. This is called dispersion of light.

notes.heading: Ultraviolet Rays
notes.text: Ultraviolet rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelength shorter than visible light but longer than X-rays. They are also called UV rays.
points: Order in spectrum: Visible light → Ultraviolet rays → X-rays.
Ultraviolet rays have wavelength shorter than visible light.
Ultraviolet rays have frequency higher than visible light.
UV rays have higher energy than visible light.
The main natural source of ultraviolet rays is the Sun.
Ultraviolet rays are also produced by electric discharge lamps, mercury vapour lamps and very hot bodies.
Ultraviolet rays are commonly divided into UV-A, UV-B and UV-C.
UV-A has relatively lower energy among UV rays and can reach Earth’s surface.
UV-B has more energy than UV-A. Some part reaches Earth and can cause tanning and sunburn.
UV-C has the highest energy among these three. Most UV-C is absorbed by the atmosphere and ozone layer.
The ozone layer absorbs most harmful ultraviolet radiation from the Sun and protects life on Earth.
UV rays are used for sterilisation because they kill germs, bacteria and viruses.
UV rays are used for sterilising water, medical instruments and operation theatres.
UV rays are used to detect special fluorescent marks on currency notes.
UV rays are useful in forensic science for detecting hidden stains, fingerprints and forged documents.
Small amounts of UV radiation help the human body produce vitamin D.
Excess ultraviolet radiation can cause sunburn, skin damage, eye damage and skin cancer.
UV rays are more energetic than visible light, so they can cause chemical changes in substances.

notes.heading: X-rays
notes.text: X-rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelength shorter than ultraviolet rays but longer than gamma rays. They have high frequency and high energy.
points: Order in spectrum: Ultraviolet rays → X-rays → Gamma rays.
X-rays have wavelength shorter than UV rays and frequency higher than UV rays.
Using c = νλ, when wavelength decreases, frequency increases.
X-rays are more energetic than ultraviolet rays.
X-rays are produced when high-speed electrons suddenly slow down after striking a metal target.
This production occurs in an X-ray tube.
Simple relation: Fast electrons + metal target → X-rays.
Heavy metals such as tungsten are commonly used as the target.
X-rays have high penetrating power.
They can pass through soft tissues like skin and muscles.
X-rays are absorbed more by bones, so bones appear clearly in X-ray images.
X-rays are used in medical diagnosis to detect bone fractures, dental problems, chest problems and foreign objects inside the body.
X-rays are used in airports and railway stations to scan luggage.
X-rays are used in industry to find cracks or defects inside metal parts. This is called industrial radiography.
X-rays are used to study crystal structure by X-ray diffraction.
X-ray diffraction helps scientists understand atomic arrangement in solids.
Excess exposure to X-rays can damage living cells.
X-rays can cause cell damage, genetic changes and cancer risk.
Lead is commonly used to block X-rays.
Protection methods include lead aprons, lead shields and controlled exposure time.
X-rays are useful because they penetrate matter, but they are dangerous in excess because they can ionise atoms and damage tissues.

notes.heading: Gamma Rays
notes.text: Gamma rays are electromagnetic waves with the shortest wavelength and highest frequency in the electromagnetic spectrum. They have the highest energy among all electromagnetic waves.
points: Order in spectrum: X-rays → Gamma rays.
Gamma rays have very small wavelength and very large frequency.
Using c = νλ, when wavelength becomes very small, frequency becomes very large.
Energy of radiation: E = hν.
Because gamma rays have very high frequency, they have very high energy.
Gamma rays are produced mainly during radioactive decay, nuclear reactions, nuclear explosions and certain cosmic processes in space.
Gamma rays come from changes inside the nucleus of an atom.
X-rays are generally produced by fast electrons outside the nucleus, while gamma rays are produced by nuclear changes.
Gamma rays have very high penetrating power.
They can pass through many materials and are more penetrating than X-rays.
Thick shields of lead or concrete are used to reduce gamma rays.
Gamma rays are used in radiotherapy to destroy cancer cells.
Gamma rays are used to sterilise syringes, surgical instruments and medical equipment.
Gamma rays can kill bacteria and insects in food items and help increase shelf life.
Gamma rays are used in nuclear physics to study atomic nuclei and nuclear reactions.
Excess gamma radiation is very dangerous.
Gamma rays can cause cell damage, DNA damage, radiation sickness and cancer risk.
Protection from gamma rays includes thick lead blocks, thick concrete walls, large distance from the source and minimum exposure time.

notes.heading: Uses of Electromagnetic Waves in Daily Life
notes.text: Electromagnetic waves are used in communication, medicine, cooking, security, astronomy and industry. Different waves have different uses because their wavelength, frequency, energy and penetrating power are different.
points: Radio waves are mainly used for communication.
Uses of radio waves: radio broadcasting, television broadcasting, mobile communication, wireless communication and walkie-talkies.
Example: FM radio transmits sound signals through radio waves.
Microwaves are used in radar, microwave ovens, satellite communication, mobile towers and GPS systems.
Example: In a microwave oven, microwaves make water molecules in food vibrate and produce heat.
Infrared rays are heat rays used in TV remote controls, night vision cameras, thermal imaging, physiotherapy and weather satellites.
Example: A TV remote sends signals using infrared rays.
Visible light is used for vision, photography, microscopes, telescopes, optical fibre communication and photosynthesis in plants.
Example: Plants use visible light from the Sun to prepare food by photosynthesis.
Ultraviolet rays are used for sterilisation, killing germs, detecting fake currency, forensic investigation and vitamin D formation in small amounts.
Example: UV lamps are used to sterilise water and medical instruments.
X-rays are used for medical imaging, detecting bone fractures, airport luggage scanning, industrial defect detection and crystal structure study.
Example: Doctors use X-rays to find fractures in bones.
Gamma rays are used for cancer treatment, sterilisation of medical equipment, food preservation and nuclear research.
Example: Gamma rays are used in radiotherapy to destroy cancer cells.

Quick table: Radio waves—radio, TV and communication; Microwaves—radar, ovens and satellite communication; Infrared rays—remote controls and thermal imaging; Visible light—vision, photography and optical instruments; Ultraviolet rays—sterilisation and fake currency detection; X-rays—medical imaging and security scanning; Gamma rays—cancer treatment and sterilisation.
Important exam point: As frequency increases, energy increases.
Radio waves have the lowest energy and gamma rays have the highest energy.

notes.heading: Energy and Momentum of Electromagnetic Waves
notes.text: Electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum. This is why sunlight heats objects, microwaves cook food, and radiation can exert pressure.
points: An electromagnetic wave has electric field E and magnetic field B.
Both electric and magnetic fields carry energy.
In vacuum, energy in the electric field is equal to energy in the magnetic field.
Therefore, half the energy is in the electric field and half the energy is in the magnetic field.
Energy density means energy per unit volume.
Energy density of electric field: u_E = (1/2)ε₀E².
Energy density of magnetic field: u_B = (1/2)(B²/μ₀).
Total energy density: u = u_E + u_B.
For electromagnetic waves: u_E = u_B.
Intensity means energy crossing unit area per unit time.
Intensity = Power/Area.
SI unit of intensity is W m⁻².
Example: Strong sunlight has more intensity than weak sunlight.
Electromagnetic waves carry momentum.
If an electromagnetic wave has energy U, then its momentum is p = U/c.
Here, p is momentum, U is energy and c is speed of light.
Since electromagnetic waves carry momentum, they exert pressure when they fall on a surface.
This pressure is called radiation pressure.
For complete absorption: P = I/c.
For complete reflection: P = 2I/c.
Here, P is radiation pressure, I is intensity and c is speed of light.
Reflection gives double pressure because momentum change is greater.

notes.heading: Important Formulae of Electromagnetic Waves
notes.text: The main formulas of this chapter are based on displacement current, speed of electromagnetic waves, electric and magnetic field relation, frequency-wavelength relation, energy, momentum and radiation pressure.
points: Displacement current: I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).
This shows that changing electric flux produces displacement current.
Ampere-Maxwell law: ∮ B · dl = μ₀(I_c + I_d).
Magnetic field is produced by both conduction current and displacement current.
Speed of electromagnetic wave in vacuum: c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀).
Numerical value: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
Relation between electric field and magnetic field: E₀/B₀ = c or E₀ = cB₀.
Wave equation relation: c = νλ.
Here, ν is frequency and λ is wavelength.
Energy of photon: E = hν.
Gamma rays have high frequency, so they have high energy.
Energy density of electric field: u_E = (1/2)ε₀E².
Energy density of magnetic field: u_B = (1/2)(B²/μ₀).
For electromagnetic waves: u_E = u_B.
Momentum of electromagnetic wave: p = U/c.
Radiation pressure for complete absorption: P = I/c.
Radiation pressure for complete reflection: P = 2I/c.
Quick formula list: I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt), c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀), E₀ = cB₀, c = νλ, E = hν, p = U/c.

notes.heading: Important NCERT Points and Exam-Oriented Notes
notes.text: The most important exam points are displacement current, Ampere-Maxwell law, transverse nature of electromagnetic waves, speed of electromagnetic waves, source of electromagnetic waves and order of electromagnetic spectrum.
points: Maxwell introduced displacement current to correct Ampere’s law.
Displacement current: I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).
Displacement current is produced due to changing electric flux.
Displacement current is not due to actual flow of charge.
Ampere-Maxwell law: ∮ B · dl = μ₀(I_c + I_d).
Magnetic field can be produced by conduction current and displacement current.
A changing electric field produces magnetic field.
A changing magnetic field produces electric field.
This continuous process produces electromagnetic waves.
Electromagnetic waves are transverse because E, B and direction of propagation are mutually perpendicular.
Speed in vacuum: c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀) = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
Light is an electromagnetic wave.
Relation between electric and magnetic fields: E₀/B₀ = c or E₀ = cB₀.
Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerated electric charges.
Charge at rest produces only electric field.
Charge moving uniformly produces electric and magnetic fields.
Accelerated charge produces electromagnetic waves.
Increasing frequency order: Radio waves < Microwaves < Infrared < Visible < Ultraviolet < X-rays < Gamma rays.
Gamma rays have maximum energy because they have the highest frequency: E = hν.
Microwaves are used in radar.
X-rays are used in medical imaging.
One-mark facts: Longest wavelength EM wave—Radio waves.
One-mark facts: Shortest wavelength EM wave—Gamma rays.
One-mark facts: Highest frequency EM wave—Gamma rays.
One-mark facts: Lowest frequency EM wave—Radio waves.
One-mark facts: Used in TV remote—Infrared rays.
One-mark facts: Used in microwave oven—Microwaves.
One-mark facts: Used to detect bone fracture—X-rays.
One-mark facts: Used for sterilisation—UV rays or Gamma rays.
One-mark facts: Visible range—400 nm to 700 nm.
One-mark facts: EM waves are produced by accelerated charges.

notes.heading: Numericals on Electromagnetic Waves
notes.text: Most NCERT-level numericals from this chapter are based on E₀ = cB₀, c = νλ, E = hν and I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).
points: Numerical 1: Given B₀ = 2 × 10⁻⁷ T. Find E₀.
Formula: E₀ = cB₀.
Answer: E₀ = 60 V/m.
Numerical 2: Given E₀ = 300 V/m. Find B₀.
Formula: B₀ = E₀/c.
Solution: B₀ = 300/(3 × 10⁸) = 1 × 10⁻⁶ T.
Answer: B₀ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ T.
Numerical 3: Given frequency ν = 6 × 10¹⁴ Hz. Find wavelength λ.
Formula: λ = c/ν.
Answer: λ = 5 × 10⁻⁷ m. This lies in the visible region.
Numerical 4: Given radio wave wavelength λ = 300 m. Find frequency ν.
Formula: ν = c/λ.
Solution: ν = (3 × 10⁸)/300 = 1 × 10⁶ Hz.
Answer: ν = 1 × 10⁶ Hz = 1 MHz.
Numerical 5: Given photon frequency ν = 5 × 10¹⁴ Hz and h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ Js. Find energy.
Formula: E = hν.
Answer: E = 3.315 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.
Numerical 6: Given dΦ_E/dt = 5 × 10¹² Vm/s and ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² C²N⁻¹m⁻². Find displacement current.
Formula: I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).
Answer: I_d = 44.25 A.
Important numerical formulas: E₀ = cB₀, B₀ = E₀/c, c = νλ, λ = c/ν, ν = c/λ, E = hν, I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).

notes.heading: Quick Revision of Electromagnetic Waves
notes.text: This revision collects the key points of the chapter without adding a separate chapter summary.
points: Ampere’s law was incomplete for a charging capacitor.
In the wire of a charging capacitor, conduction current flows.
Between capacitor plates, there is no conduction current, but the electric field changes with time.
Maxwell introduced displacement current to solve this problem.
Displacement current is due to changing electric flux: I_d = ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).
Displacement current is not due to actual flow of charge.
Ampere-Maxwell law: ∮ B · dl = μ₀(I_c + I_d).
Magnetic field is produced by conduction current plus displacement current.
A changing electric field produces magnetic field.
A changing magnetic field produces electric field.
This continuous process forms an electromagnetic wave.
Electromagnetic waves are transverse waves.
E ⟂ B and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
Speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum: c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀) = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
Light is an electromagnetic wave.
Relation between field amplitudes: E₀/B₀ = c or E₀ = cB₀.
Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerated charges.
Charge at rest produces electric field only.
Charge moving uniformly produces electric and magnetic fields.
Accelerated charge produces electromagnetic waves.
Electromagnetic spectrum from low frequency to high frequency: Radio waves → Microwaves → Infrared → Visible → Ultraviolet → X-rays → Gamma rays.
Energy increases with frequency because E = hν.
Radio waves have the lowest energy.
Gamma rays have the highest energy.
Electromagnetic waves do not need a medium and can travel in vacuum.
Electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum.
Electromagnetic waves exert radiation pressure.
Electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are in the same phase.
